title: Adaptive Reinforcement Learning for Robust Open Quantum System Control: A Multi-Task Framework with Temporal Optimization
authors: Haftu W. Fentaw, Steve Campbell, Simon Caton
abstract: We present a Multi-task Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) Reinforcement Learning framework designed for open-system quantum control across diverse Hamiltonians, which learns optimal pulse sequences while simultaneously discovering problem-specific evolution time T and number of control pulse segments N. Experimental results across 51 Hamiltonian variations demonstrate that the multi-task SAC model is able to generate control pulses that can drive a system, under environment noise, from its initial state to its target state with high fidelities, establishing essential foundations for universal quantum control applicable to realistic noisy quantum devices. Through progressive expansion of the training Hamiltonian set, we investigate if a single multi-task model trained using a given number of sample Hamiltonians can successfully accomplish state-transfer tasks for Hamiltonians drawn from the same Hamiltonian space but not encountered during training. In addition, our Robustness Infidelity Measure (RIM) analysis reveals that SAC trained policies exhibit superior robustness to pulse amplitude perturbations and decoherence rate variations compared to GRAPE-optimized controls.
summary: The paper presents a multi-task reinforcement learning framework that learns to control various quantum systems simultaneously. By optimizing both the control pulses and the timing parameters, the method achieves high-fidelity state transfer and outperforms traditional gradient-based methods in robustness against environmental noise.
main_problem: Developing a robust, unified quantum control framework that generalizes across diverse Hamiltonians and maintains high fidelity in the presence of environmental noise and hardware uncertainties.
main_result: A multi-task Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reinforcement learning agent achieves high-fidelity state transfer across 51 different Hamiltonians and demonstrates superior robustness to pulse amplitude and decoherence fluctuations compared to the GRAPE algorithm.
method: A multi-task Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reinforcement learning framework that uses a system descriptor to condition the policy and simultaneously optimizes control pulse amplitudes, total evolution time (T), and the number of pulse segments (N).
model_or_system: Open quantum systems ranging from single-qubit to three-qupi configurations, modeled using the Lindblad master equation to account for amplitude damping and dephasing noise.
key_observables: State transfer fidelity (F) and the Robustness Infidelity Measure (RIM) based on the Wasserstein distance.
important_parameters: Evolution time (T), number of pulse segments (N), decoherence rate (gamma), and pulse amplitude perturbations.
main_assumptions: The system is prepared in a pure initial state; decoherence is primarily modeled via amplitude damping; the use of Born-Markov and Secular approximations for the Lindblad equation.
figures_summary: Figure 1 compares closed-system fidelities between task-specific and multi-task models; Figure 2 shows open-system fidelity distributions; Figure 3 compares the RIM of SAC versus GRAPE; Figure 4 shows the learned distributions of temporal parameters T and N.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of robust control, describes the multi-task SAC architecture and adaptive temporal optimization, details the training strategy via progressive Hamiltonian expansion, presents benchmarks for closed and open systems, and concludes with a robustness analysis and discussion of scalability.
why_interesting: This work is highly relevant for researchers in open quantum systems as it provides a scalable, machine-learning-based approach to achieving universal control that is inherently robust to the types of noise and parameter drift encountered in realistic quantum hardware.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: Distribution of maximum state fidelities for closed-system dynamics across single-qubit (SQ), two-qubit (TQ), and three-qubit (ThQ) systems. Each box plot represents 30 independent experi- ments, demonstrating the agent’s capability to reach near-ideal fidelities across a diverse Hamiltonian set. The red diamonds denote the average fidelity for task-specific models, while the box plot repre- sents fidelity distribution for multi-task model(the horizontal green lines denote the mean fidelity).

Fig. 2: Open-system fidelity distribution across various quantum control systems. The box plots illustrate the distribution of fidelities for single-qubit (SQ), two-qubit (TQ), and three-qubit (ThQ) systems over 30 experiments per system. The horizontal orange lines denote the median fidelity.

Fig. 3: Robustness Infidelity Measure (RIM) analysis for SAC and GRAPE. Left: RIM for SAC, Right: RIM for GRAPE. SAC achieves mean infidelity of approximately 0.045 under all perturbation types, and GRAPE exhibits higher mean infidelity of approximately 0.061 under combined pertur- bations.

Fig. 4: Distribution of learned effective evolution time T (left) and effective number of control steps N (right) across 1530 experiments. The distributions show that most systems converge to relatively short evolution times (median T = 2.23, mean T = 2.87) and moderate discretization (median N = 6, mean N = 7.68). Note that, N assumes an integer value.
